speaker: interviewer
text: It is March 3rd, 1996. So when we left off, you were describing the final time that you saw your grandfather, and you mentioned that he gave you some-- some last advice.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He explained to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how to live and what not to do, and very fundamental things. But it's very sad to know that you will never see a person again. And we both know when I come back, you know, out of six weeks [INAUDIBLE] in quarantine, he'll be dead. I think that was one of the saddest things in my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was some of the advice he gave you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very fundamental. If you have to step over people, be sure not-- not to step on their back. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah? And how to treat people, and how never to let them know when you're afraid. And you know, things you-- you tell a young person.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you find that these things helped you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Very much so. Very often I think about them. And if in doubt, you know, I always think, what would grandpa say? [LAUGHS] I still do.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned your grandmother passed away a few months after that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] she lived in some place in another barrack. And then mother was notified that she died. And then she buried her. It was very sad when I died-- when he died, and I was in quarantine, my mother came to see me, to tell me that grandfather had died. And just at that moment, all the coffins were being carried past where I was looking out of the window. And she said, he's in one of them. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Terrible. I mean this is very sad.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you able to-- do you know if there was a funeral for either one of your grandparents?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a kind of a funeral, you know, but I don't remember much. I know that my grandmother was-- her coffin opened inadvertently, and an arm came out. It was also [INAUDIBLE]. But I think then, later on, they were, you know, cremated and got into urns. A small-- a small thing was said. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what else stands out in your mind about your time at Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: How people in the whole, or as a whole, are capable of being alive, staying alive, being, quote-unquote, happy, knowing that every day can be-- next day can be doomsday, but to enjoy that you are alive-- alive today. And it's very, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] very interesting to see that in retrospect. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Because some of the things which you are confronted with were very sad, be it the dying people, be it the starving children, or people who suddenly lost their minds. Not the hard work. I mean, everybody has to work for a living. But that was the saddest thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there ever a time where you didn't think you were going to make it?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I knew I wanted to be on my birthday on the palms.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned to me earlier that, while you were at Theresienstadt, that international observers came. When did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been in '44, in June. They were from Denmark and Swe-- and Sweden, and Switzerland. And the whole camp was being, like, beautified for these people. I don't know how many there were, maybe eight or 10. And suddenly there were flowers, and there were better food. And because you never know when they would be coming. And they were just officers from these particular committees. And I was confronted with them while I was working in the post office. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And for some reason-- I don't know, probably because I wore something red, they asked me questions that wasn't scheduled. And they kind of sat and stood-- half sat, half stood in front of me, and asked me all kinds of questions, which were [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] not leading-- leading questions, but you had to be very careful how to answer them. And behind me was one of the SS guys, so I had to be sure to say the right thing because otherwise, I would be in the little prison. And-- but before we started, they somehow let me know that one of them had, in their lapel, a camera, and another one had the microphone. So whatever they asked-- if-- I had to say what the Germans wanted me to say, but my face would say something different. So when they put the two and two together, they knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what-- what kind of things did they ask you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was always, did they have good food, or-- I can't remember all of it. But, you know. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Very leading and non-leading questions, you know, one after the other. They were very schooled and skilled people how to do it. And you just answered them very quickly. And just you moved your face and tell them, you know? You couldn't say no. You had to say, yes. But you let them know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You-- you had mentioned the little prison. What was the little prison and--

speaker: interviewee
text: The [INAUDIBLE] is called the [GERMAN] It was called the little prison. I'm lucky I never saw it. It was away from Theresienstadt, and it was a prison. Is was called the [GERMAN], and luckily, I never saw it on the inside.

speaker: interviewer
text: You also mentioned you had worked at the post office?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you do there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I registered all the packages and passes which came in for the inmates. There were different rules for the Czech people, and different rules for the German people. The Czech people could get rather large packages every three months. They had to have a certain stamp on it. And the Germans could send whenever they wanted to. But the German's didn't have any food. So the would-- maybe they would send some potatoes, or maybe egg powder, or something. They didn't have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And very interesting was the King of Denmark, who was very, very active in sending his subjects, every month, a package. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know whether you know the story about the King of Denmark. Do you? Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you?

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm familiar with it, but tell it.

speaker: interviewee
text: The King of Denmark decided that he would not have all the Jews be deported. And he helped to falsify papers. But there were a small percentage he could not falsify. He had to produce so and so many of them. And I think they were about 150 or 200. And he bid them goodbye. Among them, one man, his name was Pulano, and he was an officer of his inner circle. And Pulano was not allowed to take any civilian clothes. He had to come in a full uniform. And when they appeared in Theresienstadt, and Pulano came out as an officer-- as a high officer-- the Germans had to salute him. It was very strange. Of course, he got some work clothes later. And the King kept his promise that none of his people were deported to Auschwitz. And he saw to that. And each of them got a package each-- one month. Each month they got one. And I don't know how they were constructed, but in a small package was everything in it, what you needed for a month, between egg powder, and this and that, and the other. They really kept them alive-- kept them alive. Also, before the war ended, he sent his three-- two or three buses back to pick up his Jews, so that they should not get into the turmoil of the end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were working at the post office, were any of these parcels opened by the authorities?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. They had to be opened. There was a law. There was a [NON-ENGLISH], or whatever you call them. [NON-ENGLISH] they were Czech police people or something. And they had to open them proforma. Sometimes they looked into it, sometimes not. You had to show that you have-- that it's you, who it is, and that's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you were at Theresienstadt, was there any money in circulation there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. There was [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Theresien money.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was unique to the camp, to the ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course. Yes. You also could buy something. There were-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] there were little restaurants where you could buy, maybe, a watered down drink of coffee, or something. Or-- food you couldn't buy. But the money was-- was distributed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- when you worked, you got paid with this money?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you got it anyway. No, no, no. You got that money anyway whether you worked or not.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was like a ration, almost.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there any-- any form of entertainment, anything you could do?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was organized entertainment. There were musicals, operas. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There was a quartet-- string quartet. There was piano recitals. There was a man called Meyer. He played an accordion. And you knew when they were. Somehow there was-- I don't know how it was publicized. But you went to see it. It didn't cost you anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a favorite thing that you saw?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. It was all very entertaining and very-- the people who did it, they were artists. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So it was entertaining for the time it lasted. It took your mind off.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had mentioned that some people at Theresienstadt were-- were deported to Auschwitz. How frequently did these deportations take place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I know there were some in '43. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And then, 10 in '44. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I left Auschwitz in one of them, on the 10-- the 10 or 11, which went in October of '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: How were people selected for these deportations?

speaker: interviewee
text: By will. I don't know whose will. I have no idea. I was selected, because I was a half-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE]. I didn't have a father, though I had a step-father. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a government of the ghetto? Were there--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. There was a government. And an [NON-ENGLISH], as it was called. And they made the laws. And they-- these laws had to be [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] adhered to.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who enforced the laws?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was no police. But, you know, when they say you had to be home at such and such time, you went. You know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't avoid curfew or--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no, no, no, no. For what? You know. Senseless.

speaker: interviewer
text: When were you deported to Auschwitz? When did that happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: October '44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you were selected to go? Or how you were notified? What happened leading up to--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was notified. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then, it was very sad, because, since I was working for the post office, I knew I could not give any protection to my mother or her husband anymore. Because, since I worked at the post office, I gave-- I quote, end quote, gave protection. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] so this is how I got to Auschwitz. And then one transport later, my mother and her husband got to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just to go back to Theresienstadt. You had described to me before we started that, at one time, everyone, or thousands of people, were all called out to be counted.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you describe that? Do you remember first when that happened, roughly?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Novem-- on a November day. I think '43 or '44. It was cold. And each living person had to leave the ghetto, and to get to some fields. And everybody had to go with the people they lived with. And then somebody came along, really, and counted the people-- one, two, three, four, five. They wanted to know how many people they had. And it took a day and a bit. No, a day. They started early in the morning, and then they counted. And then they recounted. And it was very dark when we got home.

speaker: interviewer
text: But the bad part was, it was-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] you could only go through one person to get back into it again. It was like a gate. Can you imagine 70,000 people getting, one by one, through a gate? Panic. Absolute panic.

speaker: interviewee
text: We got hungry. We didn't get any food that day. But I don't know. They counted the people. Even those who were, you know, in the hospital had to be carried out. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] Next day was a normal life again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a uniform that you were given at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Theresienstadt? No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were able to have your own clothes?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever you able to have. Because, you know, when you-- you're in a place for that long time, and you had very little clothes left. So we came with a suitcase or two. I mean, there was nothing much you could wear, because there was nothing left.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had to borrow, you had to buy it with bread, or with whatever. And-- or if somebody had an old sweater, so you unraveled it and you made a new one from it. So it was not very fashionable. Was there a sort of black market for any kind?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Very much. Black market was-- [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I don't think there's any black market in the world which could top black market in Theresienstadt. The [INAUDIBLE] would bring in whatever people wanted from them, or ask them for. And they would bring it in. How they brought in sometimes you wouldn't-- you know, on themselves-- or how they did it. But everything was to be had. And the [INAUDIBLE] were doing it.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did-- what were you-- what was used to pay for these things?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I never needed anything badly enough. But they-- I don't think they took too much-- too much for bringing it in. But it was black market.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of things were available there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Be it a pair of shoes, or a suit, or glasses, or medication. I know medication I once got for my-- for my stepfather. No. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] What I know [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] is they did business for the people in Theresienstadt on their day off. And they met somehow in the ghetto, then, and concluded their business. But they never charged much, the [INAUDIBLE]. There was-- they had the idea that they should be nice to the people in Theresienstadt. An example is that sometimes the females had to go to the fields and work there. And everybody wanted to go to the fields, which only a few people could do. But as you left at one side-- one gate, there was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a mailbox. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And it was an unwritten rule that people could drop their letters into this mailbox, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] just as they are. And the postal offices would then forward the letters. And the Germans very soon realized that, and they wanted to have this mailbox removed, and whatever. But they couldn't do it. And then they told the [INAUDIBLE] to take the people this way to the fields, and not that way. But they wouldn't do it. Everybody came there and dropped their mail. And the Czech-- Czech mail officers would always deliver each letter. And once you were on the fields, and let's say there were 25 females, then the [INAUDIBLE] would blow the whistle. That would mean anybody can come. So friends from the Czech-- from the Czech people, would come to the fields, have also something around their head, see their friends, and they would blow-- blow the whistle twice. It means, get lost. The Germans are coming. And these people brought things in.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you mentioned October, '44, you leave Theresienstadt. How were you transported to Auschwitz? What--

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like inside of there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle cars.

speaker: interviewer
text: Describe it for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cattle car is nothing. Four walls and a roof. And you put 50 people in there, either they sit or they stand, or whatever. End of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did it take to get to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. I knew very well.

speaker: interviewer
text: They told you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was going through your mind in the cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: While I was still in Hamburg, I had a terrible-- I had a dream. And [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I remember that I went downstairs to my mother, trying to explain this dream to her. But I had to draw it. It was something like a tower made out of wood, and an awful lot of wire, barbed wire around, and guns. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And it was a terrible dream, and it took a long time for me to recuperate from that dream.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my mother, you know, ripped it apart, and we put fire to it, you know, put a match to it. And she said, it's a terrible dream. But maybe something happen-- will happen later in life. And you'd think this was a warning. So I figured, well, maybe this is where the warning comes into play. I was waiting for it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when I got there, and the doors open, that was it. So I was not so taken aback. Because I had seen it already before. I dreamt it. And It was terrible. People were crying and lamenting, and they didn't want to get out of these cattle cars. And it was pitiful. And there was our friend, Dr. Mengele, standing, in his normal way, to the right and to the left, one of his assistants. And he put his hands, similar to [INAUDIBLE], like this. And would have a thumb going this side, that, and this. This way means the other side. In Czech, [CZECH], and this one is to stay alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're taken off the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: We're taken off the train, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happens? You're lined up?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lined up.

speaker: interviewer
text: So tell me what happened.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was given a wink to do-- [NON-ENGLISH], but I turned around. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] he kind of looked at me and asked me to come back. He didn't ask. He shouted, you come back, you brat. How dare you to turn around, et cetera, et cetera. These things what you hear from people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Why did you turn around? And I said, excuse me, but you're very good-looking. And because you're so good-looking, I turned around. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he kind of smirked, and pushed-- had me pushed to that side. And that's-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that was-- the other side was the one where the people were living, you know, the 200 females out of this transport.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the transport was 1,500 people. And the 50 men they should get out of it, and 200 females. And I was one from the 200 females.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as one of the 200 females, what happened to you next?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then we got into the normal camp there in Auschwitz, where these huge barracks with-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and there were sad people. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No food. Completely [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] old beyond their years, unable to stand straight anymore. In between were the kapos, which you know what that is. And the kapos were very, very harsh, and hated everybody except the kapos. And they would hit you and [INAUDIBLE] with whips, and whatever have you. They really had all the power there. And you had to clean up. There was only dirt, but you had to clean up the dirt. We got very little food. And there was very little way to sleep, except sideways on these [? bridges-- ?] not longways, sideways. And you had an Appel. In other words, you had to be counted three, four times a day. And it was awful. Many people died. When we got there, some-- some-- you know, some corpses were lying around, but nobody paid attention.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned there was very little food. What-- what did the food consist of?

speaker: interviewee
text: Water soup. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What was in the soup, I have no idea. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Maybe you got a little piece of bread, or-- yeah. But that's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Day to day, what-- what was your routine like at Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: To stay alive. Try not to be hit by the kapos. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Try not to get too close to people who you knew will die at any minute. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I don't know. Just something general-- I have to stay alive. How do I do it? I have to stay alive. I want to see the palms on my birthday. And that's all. You see what's going on. You hear what's going on. But you cannot allow it to touch you. Because the minute it touches you, it affects you.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. We'll end tape four here, and we'll pick this up with tape number five.
